images.caption:
Figure 1:Inappropriate Disclosure in CUAs.The user asks the agent to send the office supplies list;
with access to the open shopping list and messenger.
The agent surfaces personal items alongside the work-relevant ones
in the reply to a colleague.

Figure 2:AgentCIBenchscenario-generation pipeline.An MCTS
search mutates seed scenarios into new candidates using an LLM, runs proxy agents
on them, and retains the high-reward, non-duplicate ones for the
final pool.

Table 1:State-grounded results for all fifteen agents, grouped by
model weight class and sorted by Engaged Leakage. Bootstrap 95% CIs
as faded subscripts. Shading: blue = Utility, red = Leakage / Engaged
Leakage, gray = Refusal. The 84-point Engaged-Leakage spread among
high-utility agents (U>75.0%U{>}75.0\%) shows task-completion utility does
not predict disclosure restraint.

Figure 3:Utility vs. engagement-conditioned leakage.Marker size indicates refusal rate and color indicates model family.
Dashed lines atU=75%U{=}75\%andLeng=50%L_{\mathrm{eng}}{=}50\%define four regions:
onlyClaude-Opus-4.7falls in the desirable “capable & careful”
quadrant (high utility, low leakage), while several top-utility agents cluster
in the “capable but careless” upper right.GPT-5.4achieves
comparatively low leakage mainly via a high refusal rate (large marker,
lower left). Raw leak rates in AppendixH.

header: 7Do Disclosures Persist in End-to-End UI Interactions?

images.caption:
Table 2:Engagement-conditioned leakage per failure mode.Three patterns emerge: safety-tuned models
(Opus, GPT-5.4) keep TAO and RMA low and elevate VCL; selectively
engaging models (Kimi) suppress VCL and RMA but spike on TAO; weakly
aligned models (Gemini-3.1-Pro, Qwen-3.6-Max) saturate every mode.
See AppendixJfor the full table.

Figure 4:End-to-end results forClaude-Opus-4.7andClaude-Sonnet-4.6in the OpenApps
UI environment, compared against their state-grounded baselines.
On the runs the agents complete, engagement-conditioned leakage rises
to 42.9% for Opus and 80.0% for Sonnet, at or above the
state-grounded rate in both cases. Trajectory details are in
AppendixM(Table14).

Figure 5:Real end-to-end trajectory ofClaude-Opus-4.7, interacting with the environment. The agent cycles between OpenTodos and Nora’s message thread for
13 steps, then at step 14 fills the message box with all 12 to-do
items and sends it; four inappropriate items (plumber, vet, dry-cleaner,
and an external Stripe contact) are shared.

Figure 6:Three defenses against the baseline agent,
averaged acrossClaude-Opus-4.7,GPT-5.4, andDeepSeek-v4-Pro. Leakage is the engagement-conditioned rateLengL_{\mathrm{eng}}(leak rate among non-refused runs) to remove the
refusal-rate confound.
Every defense lowers leakage and raises utility at the same time.

Table 4:Engine configuration. The same hyperparameters are used for
all four MCTS passes; only the random seed and seed pool differ across
passes.
(a)OpenTodos: mixed work and HR items.
(a)OpenTodos: mixed work and HR items.
(b)OpenMessages: Nora thread with compose box.

Table 7:The fifteen agents evaluated in the main study, grouped by
weight class. Proprietary models are queried through their official
APIs; open-weight checkpoints are served locally via vLLM. All
agents run at temperature 0.

Table 10:State-grounded leakage with 95% bootstrap CIs, refusal rate,
and engagement-conditioned leakage per agent.

Figure 12:Raw leakage vs. engagement-conditioned leakage for the
fifteen agents.Claude-Opus-4.7stays near the diagonal
(restraint, 1.7% refusal), whileGPT-5.4shows a large
vertical gap (41.9% refusal masking leakage).

Figure 14:Engagement-conditioned leakage per agent per failure mode.
VCL = visual co-location, TAO = task-ambiguity overshare,
RMA = recipient misalignment.
Rows are ordered by overall raw leakage.

Figure 15:Behaviour decomposition: each (model, scenario) cell is
classified by whether the agent completed the task and whether the
output contained a leak. Bars stack the four shares (completed clean,
completed leak, incomplete clean, incomplete leak) and sum to 100% of
the 117 scenarios. Models are ordered by raw leakage rate.
The amber segments show that even partial / non-completing
trajectories can leak.

Table 12:Per-model defense sweep. Leakage column is engagement-conditionedLengL_{\mathrm{eng}}. Every (model, defense) cell improves on the
no-defense row for the same model on both utility and engaged leakage.

Table 14:End-to-end transfer on the 50-scenario rendered-UI subset.LengL_{\mathrm{eng}}is measured over engaged runs and remains at or above
the state-grounded baseline for both models.
